content.assessment: As a no-new-policy counterfactual, this proposal has no direct fiscal cost of its own, but from a public-finance lens it does not resolve — and may not even predictably reduce — the underlying capitation-driven cost-incidence asymmetry between selective and comprehensive tracks (F-live-education_finance-05); it merely lets demographic pressure interact with existing statutory class-size and financing rules. Because Hungary's per-capita financing system continues to reward retained high-performing students at feeder schools' expense regardless of aggregate cohort size, passive contraction addresses fiscal magnitude (fewer total places, lower total expenditure on the selective track) without addressing incidence (which schools/families bear the funding loss) or the underlying selection-driven inequity (F-live-education_finance-02, F-live-education_finance-07 on persistent disadvantaged-student outcome gaps). Geographic unevenness in contraction, with urban flagship schools retaining oversubscription longer, suggests the fiscal benefits of aggregate contraction will not be evenly distributed, potentially leaving the most resource-intensive, prestige-driven selective provision least affected.
opportunities: Fiscal savings from natural contraction, if they materialize, could in principle be redirected toward compensatory equity funding (as in TP-live-t4), though no such redirection mechanism is part of this proposal as specified
strengths: Requires no new public expenditure or administrative capacity, avoiding the transition-cost and affordability risks documented for structural reform (TP-live-t3) in a system already low in overall spending share (F-live-education_finance-06)
threats: Geographically uneven contraction could concentrate remaining selective-track fiscal resources in already-prestigious urban flagship schools, worsening rather than improving cost-incidence distribution even as aggregate selective-track expenditure share falls
weaknesses: Does not alter the capitation-based incentive structure that produces resource loss for feeder schools when high performers depart (F-live-education_finance-05), so any fiscal benefit from aggregate contraction is incidental rather than targeted

No mechanism addresses the persistent achievement gap for disadvantaged students (12.1% reaching basic skill proficiency vs 16.3% EU average) or Roma early school leaving rates (58.7% vs 9.3%), meaning the value-for-money case for a wait-and-see approach given these documented gaps is weak (F-live-education_finance-07)
